frontmatter.title: Second-order and dependently-sorted abstract syntax
frontmatter.meta: 23rd Annual IEEE Symposium on Logic in Computer Science

mainmatter.p: The paper develops a mathematical theory in the spirit of categorical algebra that provides a model theory for second-order and dependently-sorted syntax. The theory embodies notions such as alpha-equivalence, variable binding, capture-avoiding simultaneous substitution, term metavariable, meta-substitution, mono and multi sorting, and sort dependency. As a matter of illustration, a model is used to extract a second-order syntactic theory, which is thus guaranteed to be correct by construction.

frontmatter.title: Classifying topoi and second-order abstract syntax

mainmatter.p: For any small category \mathscr{C}, Fiore treats \mathscr{C}-sorted abstract syntax in the functor category { \left [ \mathscr{C} , \operatorname{Pr} { \left ( \operatorname{\mathbb{L}} { \mathscr{C} } \right )} \right ]} where \operatorname{\mathbb{L}} \mathscr{C} is the free finite limit completion of \mathscr{C}. When \mathscr{C} is a set, this recovers the standard notions of second-order abstract syntax; in general, this gives a form of dependently sorted or generalized second-order abstract syntax.

Our first step will be to understand the universal property of the { \left [ \mathscr{C} , \operatorname{Pr} { \left ( \operatorname{\mathbb{L}} { \mathscr{C} } \right )} \right ]} viewed as a topos or logos in the sense of Anel and Joyal. It is instructive to first consider the universal property of \operatorname{Pr} { \left ( \operatorname{\mathbb{L}} { \mathscr{C} } \right )} as a logos. In particular, this is the free extension of the initial logos \mathbf{Set} by a diagram of shape \mathscr{C}; by this token, it is reasonable to write \mathbf{Set} { \left [ \mathscr{C} \right ]} for \operatorname{Pr} { \left ( \operatorname{\mathbb{L}} { \mathscr{C} } \right )}.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We will argue that the category of left exact cocontinuous functors \operatorname{Pr} { \left ( \operatorname{\mathbb{L}} { \mathscr{C} } \right )} \to \mathscr{E} is equivalent to the category of arbitrary functors \mathscr{C} \to \mathscr{E}, naturally in \mathscr{E}.

ol.li: By Diaconescu’s theorem, left exact cocontinuous functors out of \operatorname{Pr} { \left ( \operatorname{\mathbb{L}} { \mathscr{C} } \right )} correspond to torsors on \operatorname{\mathbb{L}} \mathscr{C}, i.e. internally flat functors out of \operatorname{\mathbb{L}} \mathscr{C}.
A functor out of a finitely complete category is flat if and only if it is left exact.
A left exact functor from the free finite limit completion of \mathscr{C} is the same as an arbitrary functor out of \mathscr{C}.

mainmatter.p: Dually, the logos \mathbf{Set} { \left [ \mathscr{C} \right ]} corresponds to the classifying topos of a \mathscr{C}-shaped diagram, which we shall write \mathbb{A} ^{ \mathscr{C} }; when \mathscr{C} = \mathbf{1} is the terminal category, we have \mathbb{A} ^{ \mathbf{1} }, the classifying topos of an object. Thus we may write \mathbf{Set} { \left [ \mathscr{C} \right ]} = \operatorname{Sh} { \mathbb{A} ^{ \mathscr{C} } } in the notation of Anel and Joyal.
Next, we shall investigate { \left [ \mathscr{C} , \mathbf{Set} { \left [ \mathscr{C} \right ]} \right ]} as a logos and as a topos.
mainmatter.ol:
As a logos, { \left [ \mathscr{C} , \mathbf{Set} { \left [ \mathscr{C} \right ]} \right ]} is the tensor product \mathbf{Set} { \left [ \mathscr{C} \right ]} \otimes \operatorname{Pr} { \mathscr{C} ^{ \mathsf{o} } }; this tensor product is inherited from the 2-category of locally presentable categories.
The logos above corresponds to the cartesian product of topoi \mathbb{A} ^{ \mathscr{C} } \times \operatorname{Tor} { \mathscr{C} } where \operatorname{Tor} { \mathscr{C} } is the classifying topos of torsors on \mathscr{C}.
mainmatter.p: As such, Fiore’s topos for \mathscr{C}-sorted abstract syntax classifies pairs of a \mathscr{C}-shaped diagram with a torsor on \mathscr{C}, i.e. a flat \mathscr{C}-shaped diagram.

It is instructive to consider what happens when \mathscr{C} is a set S, from the internal point of view. A S-shaped diagram is simply an S-indexed collection of types. On the other hand, a torsor T on S is the same as a filter on the discrete poset S. Unraveling the definitions, this amounts to an indicator { \left \langle s \right \rangle } : \Omega for each s \in S that is true for exactly one (undetermined) element of S. The indicator { \left \langle s \right \rangle } can be read as saying “the current sort is s.”
